race.grand_depart: Barcelona, Catalonia, Spain (first three stages entirely on Catalan/Spanish soil)
race.finish: Paris - Champs-Elysees, with a finale over the cobbles of Montmartre
race.overview: The 2026 Tour opens abroad in Barcelona with a 19.6km team time trial (the race's first TTT since 2019, and using the new format where every rider gets their own time) and stays in Catalonia for three days before crossing the Pyrenees. Total climbing is a massive ~54,450m - the big GC damage is deliberately loaded into the final week, with a Vosges summit at Le Markstein, a summit finish at Plateau de Solaison, a 26km individual time trial on the shores of Lake Geneva (stage 16), then the Orcieres-Merlette summit finish and a brutal back-to-back double ascent of Alpe d'Huez on stages 19 and 20 (stage 20 stacking the Croix de Fer, Telegraphe and Galibier for ~5,600m of climbing). The decisive GC battlegrounds are the two Alpe d'Huez days and the Lake Geneva ITT, with the Pyrenean summit at Gavarnie-Gedre and the uphill finish at Les Angles setting the early hierarchy; the marquee duel is Pogacar vs a Giro-winning Vingegaard.

teams.boss: Mauro Gianetti (CEO / Team Principal / General Manager)
teams.road_leader_reason: Sole GC leader; the entire squad is built to deliver him a record-equalling fifth Tour de France yellow jersey.

riders.note: Team leader; chasing a record-equalling 5th Tour title, reigning World Champion

stages.note: Grand Depart TTT past the Sagrada Familia, hilly finale climbing to the Olympic Stadium; individual times taken (no hiding place).
stages.headline: Grand Depart against the clock through Barcelona
stages.watch_for: A 19.6km team time trial that rolls past the Sagrada Familia before kicking up to the Olympic Stadium on Montjuic, so the day is decided as much by a squad's climbing legs as its aero engine. Crucially, individual times are taken, so no GC contender can shelter in the wheels and coast in. Watch the changeover discipline of the big teams and how many riders each leader still has around him when the road tilts up.
stages.nz_note: Short and sharp, so this is a rare one to watch end to end over dinner rather than skimming the highlights.

stages.note: Coastal roads then a lumpy finishing circuit over Montjuic; puncheur/breakaway day with ~2,500m climbing.
stages.headline: Punchers pounce on the Montjuic circuit
stages.watch_for: Coastal roads unwind into a lumpy finishing circuit over Montjuic with around 2,500m of climbing, the sort of terrain that shreds a bunch and lets the fast-finishing puncheurs and a determined break fight it out. The repeated drags mean pure sprinters will be in trouble and the punchy classics types come alive. Keep an eye on who's marking whom on the final ascent.
stages.nz_note: The last half hour is where it detonates, so if you're tight on time queue up the closing circuit.

stages.note: Crosses the Pyrenees with 3,850m of climbing and an uphill ski-station finish at Les Angles - first real GC test.
stages.headline: First summit showdown in the Pyrenees
stages.watch_for: The race crosses the Pyrenees with 3,850m of climbing and finishes uphill at the Les Angles ski station, the first genuine GC test of the whole Tour. This is where the overall contenders finally show their hand and the pretenders get exposed on the final drag to the line. Watch how deep each team can protect its leader and who dares the first real dig.
stages.nz_note: The marquee stage of week one, worth watching the full final climb rather than just the highlights reel.

stages.note: Rolling through the Pyrenean foothills (~2,800m), but a mostly downhill final 35km favours a breakaway.
stages.headline: Downhill run rolls the dice for the break
stages.watch_for: Rolling Pyrenean foothills stack up around 2,800m of climbing, but a mostly downhill final 35km tilts the odds firmly toward a breakaway that can build a buffer and hold off any late chase. Expect a ferocious fight to make the move of the day, because whoever gets up the road has a real shot. The descent to Foix rewards a brave, technical rider.
stages.nz_note: Break-day drama builds slowly, so the highlights package will capture the decisive move nicely.

stages.note: Transitional day into Pau; a likely bunch sprint after the opening mountains.
stages.headline: The fast men get their turn in Pau
stages.watch_for: A transitional day off the back of the mountains, running into Pau where the sprint trains will want to reassemble the bunch and set up a bunch gallop. The intrigue is whether the sprinters' teams have the legs to reel in a hopeful break after two hard days. Watch the lead-out choreography in the final kilometres.
stages.nz_note: A classic skip-to-the-last-3km stage if you're short on evening time.

stages.note: Classic Pyrenean queen-of-the-week stage finishing high at Gavarnie-Gedre.
stages.headline: Queen of the Pyrenees soars to Gavarnie-Gedre
stages.watch_for: The toughest Pyrenean stage of the week finishes high at Gavarnie-Gedre, a relentless mountain day built to crack the general classification wide open. This is prime terrain for the overall favourites to trade blows and for any weakness to be laid bare on the final ascent. Watch the pacing games early and who still has teammates when it matters.
stages.nz_note: A big one, so settle in for the final climb; this is appointment viewing.

stages.note: Flat run to Bordeaux - a prime sprinters' stage.
stages.headline: Bordeaux beckons the sprinters
stages.watch_for: A flat run to Bordeaux is about as pure a sprinters' stage as the Tour offers, so expect the fast men's teams to control the day and deliver a full-gas bunch finish. The tension is all in the final kilometre positioning and whether a breakaway can somehow survive on a wide, fast finale. Watch the lead-out trains lock horns.
stages.nz_note: One for the last-5km highlights if the day's been long.

stages.note: Dordogne flatlands; another sprint opportunity.
stages.headline: Dordogne flatlands set up another gallop
stages.watch_for: Flat roads through the Dordogne hand the sprinters a second straight opportunity, and their teams will be motivated to keep any break on a tight leash. With legs deep into the race, watch which lead-out remains sharpest and whether a rival team tries something unexpected. A likely bunch sprint, but nothing is guaranteed.
stages.nz_note: Another finish-line-only stage if you're picking and choosing tonight.

stages.note: Lumpy entry into the Massif Central suited to a breakaway before the first rest day.
stages.headline: Lumpy Massif Central teases the break
stages.watch_for: A lumpy entry into the Massif Central is tailor-made for a breakaway before the first rest day, with enough climbing to deter the sprinters and tempt the opportunists. The fight to get in the move should be relentless from the flag drop. Watch how the GC teams choose to ride, whether they let a break go clear or keep a nervous leash.
stages.nz_note: Break-day tactics reward watching a bit more than just the finish.

stages.note: First rest day.
Relentlessly up-and-down Massif Central stage into the Le Lioran resort; Bastille Day breakaway bait.
stages.headline: Bastille Day fireworks at Le Lioran
stages.watch_for: A relentlessly up-and-down Massif Central stage into Le Lioran, landing on Bastille Day, which historically lights a fire under the French riders desperate for a home win. It's perfect breakaway bait, with no summit finish but no respite either. Watch the French aggression from kilometre zero and the war to make the day's move.
stages.nz_note: Bastille Day break stages are pure theatre, so give this one a bit more of your evening.

stages.note: Flat transition through central France; sprinters back in play.
stages.headline: Sprinters resurface in Nevers
stages.watch_for: A flat transition through central France brings the fast men back into play, with the sprint teams keen to control a straightforward run to Nevers. The question is whether a stubborn break can spoil the party on a day everyone expects to end in a bunch kick. Watch the peloton's willingness to chase.
stages.nz_note: Comfortably a last-3km watch.

stages.note: Starts at the Magny-Cours motor racing circuit; flat and sprint-friendly.
stages.headline: Green light at Magny-Cours
stages.watch_for: Starting on the Magny-Cours motor racing circuit, this flat and sprint-friendly stage into Chalon-sur-Saone should tee up another mass gallop. With the Vosges looming, the sprinters know their windows are closing, so expect committed lead-out work. Watch which team times its run to the line best.
stages.nz_note: The petrolhead start is a fun touch, but the highlights are all in the finale.

stages.note: Longest stage of the race, rolling towards the Vosges - a day for the break or puncheurs.
stages.headline: The Tour's longest day rolls to Belfort
stages.watch_for: At 205.8km this is the longest stage of the race, a rolling grind toward the Vosges that suits a strong breakaway or the puncheurs when the finale bites. Fatigue plus terrain makes for an unpredictable day where the GC teams may be happy to let a break decide it. Watch the late climbs for the decisive selection.
stages.nz_note: A long one, so the highlights edit will do you a favour here.

stages.note: Short, sharp Vosges stage over multiple climbs finishing at Le Markstein; GC skirmishing likely.
stages.headline: Short, savage Vosges skirmish to Le Markstein
stages.watch_for: A short but brutally sharp Vosges stage packs multiple climbs into 155km and finishes at Le Markstein, the kind of compressed parcours that invites GC skirmishing and early aggression. Short mountain stages often produce the most explosive racing of all. Watch for the favourites testing each other well before the final climb.
stages.nz_note: Short and combustible, worth the full final hour tonight.

stages.note: Jura/pre-Alps summit finish at Plateau de Solaison to close week two.
stages.headline: Pre-Alps summit finish closes week two
stages.watch_for: The Jura and pre-Alps deliver a summit finish at Plateau de Solaison to sign off the second week, a serious mountaintop test right before the rest day. Riders on the limit have nowhere to hide on the final ascent, and the GC picture can shift sharply. Watch who commits everything knowing a rest day follows.
stages.nz_note: A proper summit finish, so settle in for the climb.

stages.note: Second rest day.
26km ITT along the shore of Lake Geneva - a pivotal race-against-the-clock for the GC.
stages.headline: Race of truth on Lake Geneva
stages.watch_for: A 26.1km individual time trial along the shore of Lake Geneva is a pivotal, pure race against the clock that can reshape the entire general classification. There's no wheel to hide behind and no teammate to pace you, just power, aerodynamics and nerve. Watch the intermediate splits to see who's gaining and who's cracking under the pressure.
stages.nz_note: TT riders go off individually, so the highlights format actually suits it; catch the key GC times.

stages.note: Valley stage into Voiron; last chance for the sprinters before the Alps explode.
stages.headline: Last dance for the sprinters before the Alps
stages.watch_for: A valley stage into Voiron is the fast men's final realistic chance before the Alps explode, so the sprint teams will be desperate to keep it together. Expect a breakaway to test their resolve on a day where motivation runs high on both sides. Watch whether the bunch times the catch or leaves it too late.
stages.nz_note: The last bunch sprint of the Tour, worth the finale even if you skip the middle.

stages.note: Return of the Orcieres-Merlette ski-resort summit finish - opens the Alpine endgame.
stages.headline: Alpine endgame opens at Orcieres-Merlette
stages.watch_for: The return of the Orcieres-Merlette ski-resort summit finish flings open the Alpine endgame, the first of a brutal closing run of mountaintop finishes. With the overall on a knife edge, the favourites must weigh attacking now against saving something for what's coming. Watch the body language on the final climb for signs of who's flying and who's suffering.
stages.nz_note: The Alps begin here, so this is where the evenings get essential.

stages.note: Short, explosive first ascent of the 21 hairpins to Alpe d'Huez.
stages.headline: Twenty-one hairpins, full gas
stages.watch_for: A short, explosive 127.9km blast to the legendary 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez, the kind of compact stage that produces all-out aggression from the gun. With little terrain to hide the effort, the GC men go toe to toe on cycling's most famous climb. Watch for early attacks and who can follow the accelerations on the switchbacks.
stages.nz_note: Alpe d'Huez, short and explosive; an unmissable evening.

stages.note: Queen stage: Col de la Croix de Fer, Telegraphe and Galibier before a second Alpe d'Huez finish - ~5,600m climbing, the GC decider.
stages.headline: Queen stage decides it all
stages.watch_for: The monumental queen stage stacks the Col de la Croix de Fer, the Telegraphe and the Galibier before a second ascent of Alpe d'Huez, roughly 5,600m of climbing and the last mountain to settle the general classification. This is the day legends are forged and dreams unravel across the highest passes. Watch how far out the decisive move comes and who has anything left on the final hairpins.
stages.nz_note: The GC decider and the climax of the whole race, so clear the evening for this one.

stages.note: Processional-then-punchy finale on the Champs-Elysees with the Montmartre cobbled climb spicing the closing laps.
stages.headline: Montmartre spices the Paris finale
stages.watch_for: The traditional processional-then-punchy run to the Champs-Elysees gets a jolt from the cobbled Montmartre climb in the closing laps, which can splinter the bunch and open the door to a puncheur rather than a pure sprinter. Watch whether the cobbles blow the finale apart or the fast men's teams manage to reassemble for a Champs-Elysees dash. A fitting, unpredictable send-off.
stages.nz_note: The Montmartre twist makes this more than a parade, worth watching the closing laps in full.
